content_blocks.text:
the intro
you are here
xxiii
“How can this be a serious Java programming book?”
“What’5 with all the graphics?”
“Can 1 actually learn it this way?”
“Do 1 smell pizza?”
We know what you’re thinking.
Your brain craves novelty. It’5 always searching, scanning, waiting for 
something unusual. It was built that way, and it helps you stay alive. 
Today, you’re less likely to be a tiger snack. But your brain’5 still 
looking. You just never know.
50 what does your brain do with all the routine, ordinary, normal 
things you encounter? Everything it can to stop them from 
interfering with the brain’5 real job—recording things that matter. It 
doesn’t bother saving the boring things; they never make it past the 
“this is obviously not important” filter.
How does your brain know what’5 important? Suppose you’re out for 
a day hike and a tiger jumps in front of you, what happens inside your 
head? 
Neurons fire. Emotions crank up. Chemicals surge. 
And that’5 how your brain knows...
This must be important! Don’t forget it!
But imagine you’re at home, or in a library. It’5 a safe, warm, tiger-free 
zone. You’re studying. Getting ready for an exam. Or trying to learn 
some tough technical topic your boss thinks will take a week, ten days 
at the most.
Just one problem. Your brain’5 trying to do you a big favor. It’5 
trying to make sure that this obviously non-important content 
doesn’t clutter up scarce resources. Resources that are better 
spent storing the really big things. Like tigers. Like the danger of 
fire. Like how you should never again snowboard in shorts.
And there’5 no simple way to tell your brain, “Hey brain, thank 
you very much, but no matter how dull this book is, and how 
little 1’m registering on the emotional richter scale right now, 1 
really do want you to keep this stuff around.”
And we know what your brain is thinking.
your brain thinks 
THIS is important.
Great. Only 
637 more dull, dry, 
boring pages.
your brain thinks 
THIS isn’t worth 
saving.
